steps.title: Block the footprint (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch a loose parallelogram for the napkin's overall footprint. Tilt it — a folded napkin lying on a counter is almost never square to the viewer, and that tilt is what gives the drawing energy.

steps.title: Notice the angles (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, look hard at the angles of your four edges. Are they really at the tilts you intended? If any edge feels too horizontal or too vertical, erase it and redraw it at the angle you actually see.

steps.title: Add the fold (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, draw a line across the napkin where it folds — roughly parallel to one of the long edges, dividing it into a wider lower half and a narrower upper flap. The fold is what makes it read as fabric rather than paper.

steps.title: Plan the soft corners (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, look at the corners of the napkin. Fabric corners aren't sharp points — they sag and round a little. Sketch a small gentle curve at each corner instead of a hard angle.

steps.title: Plan one drape (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, pick one corner of the napkin to droop slightly off the counter's edge. Sketch a soft curve where that corner falls forward. This single drape sells the whole thing as cloth.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the silhouette
steps.description: Switch to ink. Trace the napkin's outer outline in confident passes, following all four edges with their soft corners and the drooping corner. Vary your pressure if you can — heavier on the near edges, lighter where the napkin recedes.

steps.title: Ink the fold (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, draw the fold line across the napkin. Let it dip slightly in the middle rather than running ruler-straight — fabric folds sag a little under their own weight.

steps.title: Suggest the weave (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, add two or three short, light strokes along the fold and near the drooping corner to hint at soft creases. Resist drawing every wrinkle — restraint is what keeps the napkin reading as a quiet object.

steps.title: Anchor with a shadow line (ink)
steps.description: Add a short horizontal mark beneath the part of the napkin still resting on the counter to suggest contact. One small stroke is enough to ground it.
